name: pyodbc
documentation:
# pyodbc

[![Windows build](https://ci.appveyor.com/api/projects/status/github/mkleehammer/pyodbc?branch=master&svg=true&passingText=Windows%20build&failingText=Windows%20build)](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/mkleehammer/pyodbc)
[![Ubuntu build](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/actions/workflows/ubuntu_build.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/actions/workflows/ubuntu_build.yml)
[![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/pyodbc?color=brightgreen)](https://pypi.org/project/pyodbc/)

pyodbc is an open source Python module that makes accessing ODBC databases simple.  It
implements the [DB API 2.0](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249) specification but is packed with even more Pythonic convenience.

The easiest way to install pyodbc is to use pip:

    python -m pip install pyodbc

On Macs, you should probably install unixODBC first if you don't already have an ODBC
driver manager installed.  For example, using the [homebrew](https://brew.sh/) package manager:

    brew install unixodbc
    python -m pip install pyodbc

Similarly, on Unix you should make sure you have an ODBC driver manager installed before
installing pyodbc.  See the [docs](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Install)
for more information about how to do this on different Unix flavors.  (On Windows, the
ODBC driver manager is built-in.)

Precompiled binary wheels are provided for multiple Python versions on most Windows, macOS,
and Linux platforms.  On other platforms pyodbc will be built from the source code.  Note,
pyodbc contains C++ extensions so you will need a suitable C++ compiler when building from
source.  See the [docs](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Install) for details.

[Documentation](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki)

[Release Notes](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/releases)

modules.name: tests.conftest
documentation: If pyodbc has not been installed in this virtual environment, add the appropriate build
directory.  This allows us to compile and run pytest without an install step in between
slowing things down.  If you are going to use this, do not install pyodbc.  If you already
have, uninstall it.
This is useful for me for very fast testing, but I realize some people may not like it, so
I'll only enable it if PYODBC_TESTLOCAL is set.

functions.name: tests.conftest._add_to_path
documentation:
Prepends the build directory to the path so that newly built pyodbc libraries are
used, allowing it to be tested without pip-installing it.

modules.name: tests
documentation: This file is required to simplify running pytest.
Build pyodbc into the project root by running: python setup.py build_ext --inplace
Then run pytest from the root: pytest tests/postgresql_test.py

modules.name: setup
documentation: !/usr/bin/env python

modules.name: tests.mysql_test
documentation: pytest unit tests for MySQL.  Uses a DNS name 'mysql' and uses UTF-8

functions.name: tests.mysql_test.test_subquery_params
documentation: Ensure parameter markers work in a subquery

functions.name: tests.mysql_test.test_close_cnxn
documentation: Make sure using a Cursor after closing its connection doesn't crash.

functions.name: tests.mysql_test.test_rowcount_nodata
documentation:
This represents a different code path than a delete that deleted something.

The return value is SQL_NO_DATA and code after it was causing an error.  We could use
SQL_NO_DATA to step over the code that errors out and drop down to the same SQLRowCount
code.  On the other hand, we could hardcode a zero return value.

functions.name: tests.mysql_test.test_rowcount_select
documentation:
Ensure Cursor.rowcount is set properly after a select statement.

pyodbc calls SQLRowCount after each execute and sets Cursor.rowcount.  Databases can return
the actual rowcount or they can return -1 if it would help performance.  MySQL seems to
always return the correct rowcount.

functions.name: tests.mysql_test.test_row_description
documentation: Ensure Cursor.description is accessible as Row.cursor_description.

functions.name: tests.mysql_test._generate_str
documentation:
Returns either a string or bytes, depending on whether encoding is provided,
that is `length` elements long.

If length is None, None is returned.  This simplifies the tests by letting us put None into
an array of other lengths and pass them here, moving the special case check into one place.

modules.name: tests.postgresql_test
documentation: Unit tests for PostgreSQL

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test._generate_str
documentation:
Returns either a string or bytes, depending on whether encoding is provided,
that is `length` elements long.

If length is None, None is returned.  This simplifies the tests by letting us put None into
an array of other lengths and pass them here, moving the special case check into one place.

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test.test_bytearray
documentation:
We will accept a bytearray and treat it like bytes, but when reading we'll still
get bytes back.

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test.test_close_cnxn
documentation: Make sure using a Cursor after closing its connection doesn't crash.

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test.test_row_description
documentation: Ensure Cursor.description is accessible as Row.cursor_description.

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test.test_executemany_failure
documentation: Ensure that an exception is raised if one query in an executemany fails.

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test.test_cnxn_execute_error
documentation:
Make sure that Connection.execute (not Cursor) errors are not "eaten".

GitHub issue #74

functions.name: tests.postgresql_test.test_cursor_messages
documentation: Test the Cursor.messages attribute.

modules.name: tests.sqlserver_test
documentation: !/usr/bin/python

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test._get_sqlserver_year
documentation:
Returns the release year of the current version of SQL Server, used to skip tests for
features that are not supported.  If the current DB is not SQL Server, 0 is returned.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_decode_meta
documentation: Ensure column names with non-ASCII characters are converted using the configured encodings.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test._test_scalar
documentation: A simple test wrapper for types that are identical when written and read.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_decimal_e
documentation: Ensure exponential notation decimals are properly handled

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_subquery_params
documentation: Ensure parameter markers work in a subquery

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_close_cnxn
documentation: Make sure using a Cursor after closing its connection doesn't crash.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_rowcount_delete
documentation: rowcount

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_rowcount_nodata
documentation:
This represents a different code path than a delete that deleted something.

The return value is SQL_NO_DATA and code after it was causing an error.  We could use
SQL_NO_DATA to step over the code that errors out and drop down to the same SQLRowCount
code.  On the other hand, we could hardcode a zero return value.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_rowcount_select
documentation:
Ensure Cursor.rowcount is set properly after a select statement.

pyodbc calls SQLRowCount after each execute and sets Cursor.rowcount, but SQL Server 2005
returns -1 after a select statement, so we'll test for that behavior.  This is valid
behavior according to the DB API specification, but people don't seem to like it.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_retcursor_nodata
documentation:
This represents a different code path than a delete that deleted something.

The return value is SQL_NO_DATA and code after it was causing an error.  We could use
SQL_NO_DATA to step over the code that errors out and drop down to the same SQLRowCount
code.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_row_description
documentation: Ensure Cursor.description is accessible as Row.cursor_description.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_executemany_dae_0
documentation: DAE for 0-length value

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_executemany_failure
documentation: Ensure that an exception is raised if one query in an executemany fails.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_cursor_messages_with_print
documentation: Ensure the Cursor.messages attribute is handled correctly with a simple PRINT statement.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_cursor_messages_with_fast_executemany
documentation: Ensure the Cursor.messages attribute is set with fast_executemany=True.

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_cursor_messages_with_stored_proc

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.test_too_large
documentation: Ensure error raised if insert fails due to truncation

functions.name: tests.sqlserver_test.get_sqlserver_version
documentation: Returns the major version: 8-->2000, 9-->2005, 10-->2008

Returns either a string or bytes, depending on whether encoding is provided,

If length is None, None is returned.  This simplifies the tests by letting us put None into
